ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried rice as a generic category. No Schezwan-specific or Indo-Chinese variant code exists; closest parent match documented.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (carrot, capsicum, spring onion, cabbage, peas)
Schezwan sauce (dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Schezwan Vegetarian Fried Rice weighs approximately 400 g, providing around 550 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried rice with vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB entry for Schezwan vegetarian fried rice exists. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore fried rice (vegetable) entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 'Rice, fried, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 169957) and USDA vegetable fried rice analogues. Schezwan sauce sodium contribution estimated from HPB condiment data. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against these sources and found broadly consistent; retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Schezwan vegetarian fried rice. GI of 56 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice fried rice (GI ~54–58, medium category). The Schezwan sauce adds a small amount of sugar and vinegar; vinegar has a mild GI-lowering effect, partially offset by added sugar. Net GI estimated at 56 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 85.0) / 100 = 47.6 g GL for a 400 g plate (85 g available carbohydrate = 21.25 g/100g × 400 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g (1200 mg per 400 g plate) driven by Schezwan sauce soy-sauce base; low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced Schezwan sauce or a low-sodium variant, as a full 400 g plate delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium — 52% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg — with insufficient potassium to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (56) with high glycemic load per serving (GL ~47.6) due to large white-rice portion; low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce portion size to 150–200 g, substitute brown rice for white rice to lower the glycemic response, and increase the vegetable-to-rice ratio to reduce the overall glycemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (300 mg/100 g) and refined carbohydrate load; low saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g) and zero trans fat are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, this dish is acceptable in small portions (150–200 g) with reduced Schezwan sauce to lower sodium; the low saturated fat and absence of trans fat are positive attributes, but the high sodium and refined carbohydrate content warrant caution at full serving size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white rice promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; nightshade-derived chilli and capsicum in Schezwan sauce may aggravate solanine-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should substitute brown rice for white rice to reduce refined carbohydrate load, monitor for symptom aggravation from nightshade ingredients (chilli, capsicum) in Schezwan sauce, and request reduced sauce to lower sodium below 400 mg/100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Schezwan Vegetarian Fried Rice is an Indo-Chinese style dish popular in Singapore and across South and Southeast Asia, consisting of cooked white rice stir-fried at high heat with mixed vegetables — typically carrot, capsicum, spring onion, cabbage, and green peas — and a bold, spicy Schezwan sauce made from dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, vinegar, and a small amount of sugar. The dish is entirely plant-based and halal-certified in most Singapore restaurant contexts. The cooking method is wok stir-frying (Chinese: 炒, chǎo), which imparts a characteristic smoky 'wok hei' aroma. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 4.5 g fat (of which 0.75 g is saturated), and 21.25 g carbohydrate with 1.5 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg/100 g due to the soy-sauce-based Schezwan sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 47.6 per standard 400 g plate. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals with hypertension (high sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI, high GL, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (high sodium, refined carbohydrate). Substituting brown rice and reducing sauce quantity significantly improves the nutritional profile. The dish is commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner and is widely available at Indian-Chinese, hawker, and fusion restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Schezwan fried rice?
Can diabetics eat Schezwan fried rice?
Schezwan fried rice vs plain fried rice nutrition
What is Schezwan sauce made of?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for Schezwan vegetarian fried rice exists. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore vegetable fried rice entries and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried rice analogues. Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with these sources. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice data. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this Indo-Chinese vegetarian fried rice preparation is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried rice entries as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore database; estimated ~150 mg/100 g in clinical commentary but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the adapted HPB/USDA sources for this dish category. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — vegetable-oil-based stir-fry with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with HPB Singapore fried rice data). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). Cultural note: the input record lists primary_cuisine as Turkish and ethnic_tags as Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma, which is inconsistent with the dish identity; this appears to be a restaurant-catalogue artefact. The dish is correctly classified as Indo-Chinese cuisine originating in Singapore. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (56 × 85.0 g available carbohydrate per 400 g plate) / 100 = 47.6.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected cuisine from 'Turkish' to 'Indo-Chinese' based on dish identity; added mandatory Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); populated full ontology section with honest approximate matches and gap notes; added GI estimate from University of Sydney GI Database analogue; added all three required health_context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes with correct human_condition_name; expanded natural_language_description to >200 characters; documented all -1 fields and confirmed-zero fields in notes; verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with hard gate G5 requirements, as 'approximate' is not a valid enum value. All other aspects of the record were already compliant.
